title: The World as a Living Book — Ficino, Hermes, and the Recovery of Divine Order
author: Hermes Trismegistus / Marsilio Ficino

body.text: Ficino and Hermes share a single premise. The universe is a book written by Mind. To read it is an act of devotion. The ordinary person sees things arranged. The Hermetic philosopher sees how they are ordered, governed, caused, and directed. The first re-rendering: the world takes shape as intelligible pattern where once there was only accident — a lattice of meaning scored into everything.

For there can be no Religion more true or just, then to know the things that are; and to acknowledg thanks for all things, to him that made them. Be Pious and Religious, O my Son; for he that doth so, is the best and highest Philosopher; and without Philosophy, it is impossible ever to attain to the height and exactness of Piety or Religion. But he that shall learn and study the things that are, and how they are ordered and governed, and by whom, and for what cause, or to what end, will acknowledg thanks to the Workman, as to a good Father, an excellent Nurse, and a faithful Steward, and he that gives thanks shall be Pious or Religious, and he that is Religious shall know both where the truth is, and what it is, and learning that, he will be yet more and more Religious.

Knowledge moves toward gratitude the way a river moves toward the sea. To know a thing truly is to know its origin, its governor, its end. The surface of a thing is the first layer. Hermes asks for the second: who caused it, how is it governed, toward what end does it tend? The world is given, and giving awakens thanks.

For never, O Son, shall, or can that Soul, which while it is in the Body lightens and lifts up it self to know and comprehend that which is Good and True, slide back to the contrary: For it is infinitely enamored thereof, and forgotteth all Evils; and when it hath learned and known its Father and Progenitor, can no more Apostatise or depart from that good. And let this, O Son, be the end of Religion and Piety; whereunto when thou art once arrived, thou shalt both live well, and die blessedly, whilist thy soul is not ignorant whether it must return, and flie back again. In this only, O Son, is the way to the Truth, which our Progenitors travelled in; and by which, making their journey, they at length attained to the good.

The soul that sees the Good is changed by the seeing. Truth enters through love. The soul sheds the pretence that forgetfulness is possible. Beauty matters in the Ficinian world because it is the first mercy of metaphysics — a gravid pull toward what the soul has forgotten. The soul must become capable of being infinitely enamoured by the Good. Love carries knowledge inward. Knowledge alone stays on the surface.

All things that are are moved onely that which is not is unmoveable. Every Body is changeable. Not every body is dissolveable. Some Bodies are dissolveable. Every living thing is not mortal. Not every living thing is immortal. That which may be dissolved is also corruptible. That which abides always is unchangeable. That which is unchangeable is eternal. That which is made but once, is never corrupted neither becomes any other thing. First, God; Secondly, the World; Thirdly, Man. The World for Man, Man for God. Of the Soul; that part which is Sensible is mortal, but that which is Reasonable is immortal.

Three orders. God, world, man. Nested inside one another. The soul stands between — part sensible and mortal, part reasonable and immortal. From this seam it can descend into confusion or ascend through affinity. It can become merely reactive, or it can become musical. Ficino's art is the art of making it musical — whetting the ear to hear what order sounds like.

If the world is ordered by Mind, then colour, sound, planet, herb, and prayer are relations inside that Mind. The soul is healed by learning to stand inside the pattern again. Beauty is the method. Spiritus is the medium. The cosmos is the teacher.
